definition: A stop loss is a predefined price level to automatically close a trade and limit losses. It is placed beyond key levels such as swing highs/lows, order block edges, or liquidity sweep points. Placement balances risk-to-reward and avoids premature stop-outs from market noise. The stop loss also determines position size based on fixed dollar risk.
identification_rules: For long trades, place stop loss below a significant swing low or the lower end of the order block.
For short trades, place stop loss above a significant swing high or the upper end of the order block.
When entering at the mean threshold of an order block, keep the stop at the same swing high/low rather than tightening to the order block edge.
After a break of structure in the trade direction, trail the stop: for shorts, reposition above the previous swing high; for longs, below the previous swing low.
For sell trades, place stop loss above the order block or the wick of the candlestick that created the order block.
For buy trades, place stop loss below the order block or the wick of the candlestick that created the order block.
When the lower time frame order block is very small, use a slightly larger stop-loss zone to prevent being stopped out by normal market fluctuations.
When the lower time frame order block is very large, either use a smaller stop zone just outside the order block or move the entry closer to the middle of the zone to improve risk-to-reward.
Place the stop loss at a level that keeps the risk-to-reward ratio at least 1:2 when setting initial targets.
For short trades, place the stop loss above the highest point of the recent candles, specifically above the engulfing candle's high.
For long trades, place the stop loss below the lowest point of the reaction candle that precedes the engulfing breakout candle.
The stop loss level should be set at the close of the engulfing candle, aligned with the breakout entry.
In FVG entry, place stop loss outside the liquidity sweep extreme point that triggered the entry.
In Breaker Block trading, option 1: place stop loss tight against the Breaker Block low (bullish) or high (bearish); option 2: place stop loss outside the nearest swing high/low.

In Order Block trading, option 1: place stop loss outside the opposite edge of the order block; option 2: place stop loss outside the nearest swing high/low and adjust entry toward invalidation point (e.g., mean threshold) to improve R:R.
If Breaker Block and Fair Value Gap overlap (Unicorn Model), stop loss can be placed outside the FVG or Breaker Block boundary.
In Candle Three Closure entry, switch to lower time frame (e.g., 15 min), identify the most recent protected swing low, and place stop loss below it.
Determine stop loss in points from entry, then calculate position size: contracts = target risk amount ÷ (point value × stop loss points).
trading_implication: Stop loss placement directly controls risk-to-reward and position size. A tight stop (e.g., order block edge) yields higher R:R but risks being wicked out on still-valid trades. A wider stop (e.g., swing high/low) is safer but requires a farther target for the same R:R. The preferred approach is to keep the stop on the swing and adjust the entry closer to invalidation. Trailing the stop after a trend move or moving to breakeven upon hitting initial targets locks in profits and reduces risk.
common_mistakes: Placing the stop on the order block low/high to improve R:R, then getting wicked out on a single tick even though the trade was never truly invalidated.
Moving the invalidation point closer to the entry instead of moving the entry closer to a fixed invalidation point.
Failing to trail the stop loss after a market structure break, risking giving back unrealized profits.
Placing the stop loss too tight, causing premature stop-outs from normal market noise or minor retracements.
Placing the stop loss too wide (e.g., beyond the entire large order block without adjustment), reducing the risk-to-reward ratio.
Not adjusting stop loss placement for the specific trading method (e.g., order block size, candle breakout area), leading to poor risk-to-reward.
Using fixed contract size instead of dynamic position sizing based on stop loss points, causing inconsistent risk per trade.
In futures prop accounts, calculating risk based on total account size rather than drawdown limit, leading to rapid account blowout after consecutive losses.
Entering without waiting for model signals (e.g., Candle Two/Three Closure), resulting in stop loss placement lacking structural support.
merge_notes: All four input cards agree on school: Risk Management. No school conflict. Definitions and rules were complementary; the unified card synthesizes all identification rules from each project, preserving special cases (e.g., Candle Three Closure, Unicorn Model, engulfing candle rules).
